title: Research: is the pinned type move analysis formally specified and proven sound?
description: pinned types (types with interior pointers that cannot be moved after construction) are described as having a 'compiler-checked' no-move guarantee. Interior pointers are inherently unsafe: if the move analysis has a bug, the program has a use-after-free. The spec does not cite a formal specification of the move analysis, does not describe how interior pointers interact with the region system, and does not explain how pinned interacts with unsafe blocks. Research needed: (1) Is there a formal model of pinned type safety? (2) Is there a proof that no safe Ferrum program can move a pinned type after construction? (3) How does pinned interact with unsafe code that copies raw pointer values?
close_reason: Specified pinned type semantics: exhaustive move definition, drop guarantee, automatic noalias suppression (no UnsafePinned needed), generic instantiation failure instead of Unpin, unsafe code contract. Design decision note explains Rust's UnsafePinned lesson and why first-class type property avoids it.
